event: Archaic (pre-ceramic) Amerindian peoples migrating through the Lesser Antilles are believed to have visited or seasonally occupied Montserrat, leaving early traces of human presence.
Saladoid agriculturalists, Arawakan-speaking peoples originating in South America's Orinoco region, establish ceramic-using settlements on Montserrat.
Post-Saladoid Amerindian cultures continue to occupy the island, leaving pottery, shell tools, and village sites such as those later found at Trants.
Island Carib (Kalinago) peoples become the dominant Amerindian group in the region, calling the island Alliouagana, meaning 'land of the prickly bush.'
Christopher Columbus sights the island during his second voyage and names it Santa Maria de Montserrate after the Montserrat monastery near Barcelona, Spain.

Irish Catholic settlers, many fleeing religious persecution in the Protestant English colony of Saint Kitts and from Virginia, establish the first European colony on Montserrat under English authority.
Sugar cultivation expands and enslaved Africans are increasingly imported, beginning the plantation economy that would define the island for centuries.
Montserrat develops a distinctive blend of Irish and African culture as Irish indentured servants and enslaved Africans labour together on the plantations.
France briefly captures Montserrat during the Second Anglo-Dutch War before it is returned to England under the Treaty of Breda.
A French force raids Montserrat during the War of the Spanish Succession, plundering plantations and carrying off enslaved people.
Enslaved Africans plan an uprising for Saint Patrick's Day on 17 March, but the conspiracy is betrayed and brutally suppressed by the planters.
France again occupies Montserrat during the American Revolutionary War period before it is restored to Britain by the 1783 Treaty of Paris.
A French naval squadron raids Montserrat and exacts a ransom from the colony, the last major foreign attack on the island.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, and Montserrat's enslaved population enters a transitional apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation takes effect, ending the apprenticeship system and freeing the formerly enslaved labourers of Montserrat.
British philanthropist Joseph Sturge purchases an estate and founds the Montserrat Company, promoting lime cultivation and offering land and wages to freed workers.
Montserrat is incorporated into the Federal Colony of the Leeward Islands as a British administrative unit.
A powerful hurricane devastates Montserrat, severely damaging the lime industry and the island's economy.
Sir George Martin's lime business and the decline of plantation agriculture prompt economic shifts, while cotton becomes a leading export crop.
Universal adult suffrage is introduced, allowing all adults on Montserrat to vote for the first time in elections to the legislative council.
The Leeward Islands federal colony is dissolved and Montserrat becomes a separate colony administered through the new arrangements.
Montserrat joins the short-lived West Indies Federation, which sought to unite Britain's Caribbean colonies.
The West Indies Federation collapses, and Montserrat reverts to direct status as a British Crown colony.
Many neighbouring islands attain associated statehood, but Montserrat chooses to remain a British dependent territory with its own constitution.
AIR Studios Montserrat, founded by Beatles producer George Martin, opens and attracts world-famous musicians who record landmark albums on the island.
Hurricane Hugo strikes Montserrat with catastrophic force, destroying most buildings and forcing the closure of AIR Studios.
The long-dormant Soufriere Hills volcano begins erupting in July, beginning a prolonged crisis that would transform life on the island.
Pyroclastic flows from the Soufriere Hills volcano destroy the capital, Plymouth, and kill nineteen people, leading to evacuation of the southern two-thirds of the island.
More than half of Montserrat's population emigrates, chiefly to the United Kingdom, as the volcanic exclusion zone renders much of the island uninhabitable.
Brades, in the safe northern zone, becomes the de facto capital and seat of government as Plymouth remains buried and abandoned.
A major dome collapse at Soufriere Hills produces one of the largest eruptions of the crisis, sending ash and debris over the island.
John A. Osborne Airport opens at Geralds in the north, replacing the W. H. Bramble Airport that had been destroyed by the volcano in 1997.
The Soufriere Hills volcano produces another large dome collapse and explosive activity, reaffirming the ongoing hazard in the southern exclusion zone.
A new Montserrat constitution comes into force, updating the relationship with the United Kingdom and expanding local rights and governance.
Little Bay in the north is designated for development as the site of a future new capital town to replace the lost city of Plymouth.
Scientists report a prolonged pause in eruptive activity at Soufriere Hills, though the volcano continues to be closely monitored.
Montserrat, as a British Overseas Territory, manages the COVID-19 pandemic with strict border controls that limit cases on the island.
